entry.title: Batch Management in SAP LGM
entry.content:
Batch Management is used to track and manage materials in batches (lots) that share the same characteristics, such as Shelf life expiration date, Batch status, Country/region of origin, Date of manufacture etc.

This is needed to ensure Traceability, Quality Control, Shelf Life and Expiry Management, Inventory Differentiation, Warehouse and Production operations and also it helps during product recalls and are needed across a variety of Industries like Pharmaceuticals, Food & Beverage, Chemicals, Cosmetics, Medical Devices, Agriculture/Seeds and Manufacturing with lot-controlled materials.

Considering the importance of this feature SAP has introduced it in SAP LGM also. This blog covers an overview of how the functionality is designed as per current development.

We will cover it under following topics

  1. Batch Creation
  2. Stock Upload without Purchase Order
  3. Logistics Processes with Batch Management (Inbound & Outbound)
  4. Exception – How to handle scenario with missing Batch in S/4 document.

Lets start with the first topic

Batch Creation

  1. Batch Creation in S/4 system and transfer to LGM
    • Activate the Product as Batch Relevant and Define Batch in S/4 with key details like Date of Manufacture / SLED etc.

  • Within SAP LGM system using the App - Manage Replication Configuration, create a Replication variant that will be used for replicating batches from S/4 system to LGM

  • Using the App - Schedule Batch Replication configure the job to schedule the replication. This can be based on a schedule or even immediate

  • Once the Batches are replicated, they can be seen using the App - Display Batches

Let's now look at the Stock upload process without the Purchase Order

The Process starts with MIGO based GR posting in S/4 system. This creates Delivery in the S/4 system with batch details.

Based on the set up configurations a Logistical Demand is created and the batch details are available at the item level.

The corresponding Inbound Warehouse request also contains the batch details and once the warehousing process is completed the stock is updated back into S/4 system.

 

 

Let's now look at the Inbound and Outbound process:

Inbound Delivery based Batch Process

Once the Batch has been created in S/4 system and replicated in LGM, create the PO with the Batch Managed Product. The Batch details can either be provided in the PO or in Delivery.

Create Delivery for the PO with batch details.

On saving the Delivery, Logistical Demand is created in SAP LGM and the Batch Details are also seen in the document item.

 

 

The details also flow thru into the Inbound Warehouse request which can then be processed further to complete the warehouse processing.

 

Outbound process with Batch Management

The Outbound process starts with the creation of a Sales order and the Outbound Delivery within S/4 with a Batch managed product and Batch details.

Once the delivery is distributed it creates a Logistical Demand in SAP LGM. The Batch can be seen here at the item level.

In the above scenario this starts the warehouse processes and the Batch details flow into the Outbound Warehouse Request and also the Packing Work Center as can be seen below:

Once the Packing is completed, we can use the Monitor Handling Units app to view the batch details in the Handling Unit.

Once the Warehouse processes are completed the Goods Issue is posted and the stock details for the Batch Managed Product are updated in the S/4 system.

 

Now let's look at the LGM system behavior in the case where we have Missing Batch Details in a Delivery with Batch Managed Product

In this scenario we have a Batch Managed Product but have not provided the batch details in S/4 while creating the delivery. The Delivery is distributed to the S/4 system and is not editable in S/4 system.

Since the product is Batch Managed with missing details, the LGM system validates the data and the Logistical Demand is created with an Error Status as shown below.

To correct the issue, this Logistical Demand can be "Cancelled".

The Cancellation triggers an Integration task to "Decline" the Inbound Delivery in this case which then updates the Delivery in S/4 system.

The system then updates the Good Movement Status to "Completed" with a "Delivered Quantity" as "0".

We can now create a new Inbound Delivery with the required batch details and then transfer the same to LGM correctly with the Batch details.

That's all on the topic of Batch Management in SAP LGM.

Hope this helps.

For More Details on this topic, please refer to the below Links

What's New Viewer - SAP Logistics Management | SAP Help Portal

Batch Management | SAP Help Portal

Batch Replication | SAP Help Portal

Display Batches | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: How I Built an AI-Powered ABAP Code Reviewer Using Google Gemini and SAP BTP
entry.content:
How I Built an AI-Powered ABAP Code Reviewer Using Google Gemini and SAP BTP

Introduction

Generative AI is rapidly changing software development, and as an SAP ABAP developer, I wanted to understand how these technologies could be applied to solve real problems within the SAP ecosystem.

One activity that every ABAP developer is familiar with is code review. Before code reaches production, it usually goes through peer reviews, ATC checks, or Code Inspector validations. While these tools are excellent at identifying issues, they often don't explain why a particular coding practice is problematic or how it can be improved.

This inspired me to build a simple AI-powered ABAP Code Reviewer using Google Gemini, Python, and SAP Business Application Studio on SAP BTP. The objective wasn't to replace ATC or Code Inspector, but to provide developers with easy-to-understand explanations and practical improvement suggestions.

In this blog, I'd like to share how I built the application, the technologies I used, the challenges I faced, and the lessons I learned during the process.

The Idea

The application is intentionally simple.

A developer pastes an ABAP program into a web page, clicks Review, and receives an AI-generated analysis covering:

  • Performance issues

  • Modern ABAP best practices

  • Code quality improvements

  • Object-Oriented ABAP recommendations

  • Security considerations

  • S/4HANA and Clean Core guidance

  • Overall code quality rating

The primary focus is helping developers understand why an issue exists instead of simply identifying it.

Technology Stack

The project uses the following technologies:

  • Python

  • Flask

  • Google Gemini 2.5 Flash

  • HTML, CSS and JavaScript

  • SAP Business Application Studio on SAP BTP

Python and Flask were used to build the backend API, while SAP Business Application Studio provided a convenient cloud-based development environment.

Overall Architecture

The application follows a straightforward request-response flow:

  1. The developer enters ABAP code in the web interface.

  2. The frontend sends the code to a Flask API.

  3. The backend combines the code with a carefully designed system prompt containing SAP coding guidelines.

  4. Google Gemini reviews the code.

  5. The generated review is returned and displayed in the browser.

Although simple, this architecture allowed me to focus on understanding Generative AI rather than building a complex application.

Building the Application

Step 1 – Setting up the environment

I created a Python project in SAP Business Application Studio and installed the required dependencies, including Flask and the Google Gemini SDK.

Insert a code sample showing the installation commands here using the SAP Community code editor.

Step 2 – Prompt Engineering

The most important part of the application wasn't the Flask code—it was the prompt.

Instead of asking Gemini to "review this code," I provided structured SAP-specific instructions covering areas such as:

  • Performance optimization

  • Modern ABAP syntax

  • Object-Oriented ABAP

  • Security checks

  • S/4HANA development practices

  • Clean Core recommendations

This significantly improved the consistency and relevance of the generated reviews.

Insert a representative excerpt of your SYSTEM_PROMPT here using the code sample feature.

Step 3 – Building the Review API

The Flask API exposes a /review endpoint.

When the user submits ABAP code, the API:

  • validates the request,

  • combines the code with the system prompt,

  • sends the request to Google Gemini,

  • returns the generated review as JSON.

Despite being only a few lines of code, this endpoint forms the core of the application.

Insert the Flask API code here using the code sample feature.

Step 4 – Creating the User Interface

For the frontend, I kept the interface intentionally simple.

The page contains:

  • a text area for entering ABAP code,

  • a Review button,

  • an output section that displays the AI-generated review.

My objective was to focus on the AI integration rather than frontend complexity.

 

Demo

 

Challenges I Faced

The biggest challenge during development was integrating the latest Google Gemini SDK.

While following online examples, I noticed that many tutorials used the older google.generativeai package, which has since been replaced by the newer google.genai package. Updating the project to the latest SDK and resolving API compatibility issues required some experimentation.

This experience also highlighted how quickly AI libraries evolve and the importance of referring to the latest documentation.

What I Learned

This project taught me much more than simply calling an AI model.

Some of my biggest learnings were:

  • Building Python web applications using Flask.

  • Developing applications within SAP Business Application Studio.

  • Integrating Generative AI into enterprise applications.

  • Understanding how prompt engineering influences AI output.

  • Appreciating how SAP domain knowledge can improve AI-generated recommendations.

Future Improvements

This project is only the first version.

Some enhancements I plan to explore include:

  • Migrating from Flask to FastAPI.

  • Separating AI prompts from application logic.

  • Adding deterministic rule-based checks before invoking the LLM.

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using SAP documentation.

  • GitHub Pull Request integration for automated code reviews.

  • Support for additional SAP development guidelines.

Conclusion

This project showed me that combining SAP expertise with Generative AI can produce practical developer tools.

Rather than replacing existing SAP tools such as ATC or Code Inspector, AI can complement them by explaining findings, suggesting improvements, and helping developers adopt modern development practices more quickly.

If you're an SAP developer interested in Generative AI, I encourage you to start with a small project that solves a real problem you encounter in your daily work. Building something practical is one of the best ways to learn.

The complete source code for this project is available on GitHub:

https://github.com/AryaRanjan3101/sap-ai-code-reviewer

I'd be happy to hear your suggestions or ideas for improving the project.

entry.title: View Recommended Notes in SAP Cloud ALM Applications
entry.content:
If you've worked with the Maintenance Planner, you're likely already familiar with its Notes recommendation feature. The good news, This feature has now been brought into SAP Cloud ALM.

Here's the big picture: in Landscapes – Design & Visualization (LDV), you can calculate recommended security notes on demand. LDV then pushes those notes to Configuration & Security Analysis (CSA), where a daily background job keeps everything up to date automatically. The result? All your security note recommendations are:

📍Available in the same tenant as your landscape
🔄Refreshed daily — no manual effort required
🎯Always focused on what still needs attention — once a note is implemented, it drops off the list in the next cycle

In this blog, we'll walk you through how it all works and what you need to set up. New to SAP Cloud ALM? This is a great place to start.

The Two Apps at the Center of This

Landscapes – Design & Visualization (LDV) 

LDV gives you a graphical overview of your entire system landscape — cloud, on-premise, and private cloud — all in one place. You can explore systems, clients, logical connections, and business service groups, along with the relationships between them. Beyond visualization, LDV also helps you stay ahead of security risks by enabling you to calculate recommended security notes directly.

📖 For the user guide, refer to the SAP Help Portal.

Configuration & Security Analysis (CSA)

CSA is your one-stop shop for monitoring the technical configuration and security posture of your SAP landscape. It collects configuration data from ABAP-based systems and cloud services, organizing it into Data Stores — pre-validated, categorized buckets, each tagged with a compliance status.

📖 For a deeper dive, check out: Insights on Configuration and Security Analysis with SAP Cloud ALM. For the user guide, refer to the SAP Help Portal.

How They Connect

Here’s the flow under the hood:

  1. LDV fetches applicable recommended security notes for your on-premise and private cloud systems from the Maintenance Planner API, using a configured Technical S-user.
  2. LDV pushes these notes into the CSA data store ABAP_RECOMMENDED_NOTES_STATUS, where they’re ready for you to review.
  3. A scheduled background job runs once daily per tenant, keeping everything in sync automatically.

Prerequisites

Before getting started, make sure the following are in place:

  1. CALM tenant — Set up and connected per the Help Portal setup guide.
  2. CSA access — Your user has the required roles to access CSA and its data stores.
  3. LDV access — You can open LDV and see your systems listed. (Requires at least read access to Landscape Management.)
  4. Technical S-user — A Technical S-user (not a dialog S-user) must be configured in LDV with valid credentials. Note that this might be separate from the S-user you may have already maintained for other use cases in CALM. For setup details, see the SAP Help Portal. If you're using CCC, assign the S-user to the leading customer number.
  5. Role assignment — To view security notes in LDV, assign the Security Notes Viewer role under Landscape Design in the User Management app. For details, see the SAP Help Portal.

💡Tip: After maintaining credentials, allow one day for the daily background job to run before expecting notes to appear in CSA.

How to View the Notes in CSA

  1. Open the CSA – Data Stores app.
  2. Go to the Store Browser.
  3. Select the technical system you want to check.
  4. Search for the store ABAP_RECOMMENDED_NOTES_STATUS.
  5. Click the store to view the notes.
  6. Check the "Valid Since" date to confirm the data is current.

How to View the Notes in LDV

Note: Security notes can be calculated only for on-premise and private cloud systems.

  1. From the Landscapes Overview screen, open the Systems or Groups tile.
  2. Select the system or group you want to check. Multiple selection of systems is allowed but not for group.
  3. Click Calculate Security Notes to load the applicable notes. For groups, only valid systems will show results.
  4. Click any system number or note number to see its details.
  5. Use the Hot News toggle, filters, or search to narrow down the list.

 

Working with Corporate Customer Hierarchies (CCC)

If your organization uses a Corporate Customer Hierarchy (CCC) to link multiple customer numbers under a single corporate structure, CSA fully supports viewing Recommended Security Notes across all systems in the hierarchy.

The only requirement: ensure the Technical S-user of the leading customer number is maintained in LDV. Notes will then flow in for all systems across your customer hierarchy automatically.

📌 For more details on CCC, refer to:

  • SAP Note 2632518
  • SAP Note 2691631

 Wrapping Up

With the right setup in place, recommended security notes for your entire SAP landscape are just a few clicks away — consolidated, current, and all in one place.

Happy patching! 🔧

Many thanks to @Jayeta   for collaborating  and contributing to this blog.

Reference Links : 

Keeping SAP System Landscape Secure and Up-to-Date With SAP Notes Recommendations in Maintenance Planner

Note-Oriented Discovery of Maintenance Planner for SAP Security Notes

Exploring SAP Cloud ALM and its functionalities

entry.title: Re: handshake_failure
entry.content:
The target website support only WEAK ciphers, which are regarded as deprecated.

  • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
  • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256

CPI doesn't support such weak ciphers anymore, as of:
https://me.sap.com/notes/3694052

entry.title: Re: Unable to edit highest fiscal period to 2027 from 202...
entry.content:
Good day @Venkataraman_B 

 

Thank you for your question

In Public cloud, the Highest Fiscal Year in the ledger settings for Asset Accounting can normally not be reduced from 2027 t0 2026 after fiscal year 2027 has already been opened for company codes containing assets.

The highest open fiscal year can be entered manually only when there are no assets in the corresponding company code. Once you have activated Asset Accounting and assets exist, the fiscal year is advanced through the fiscal year change rather than it being freely edited backward.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/8e71a76adeb84940b785936842cc7574.html?locale=en-US

Here are areas you should check:

In Make Company Code settings – Asset Accounting Specific and select the relevant company code and ledger.

Ensure you check the following:

The Highest Fiscal Year currently shows 2027

Whether assets already exist for the company code.

Whether the fiscal year change to 2027 was executed.

Whether any asset postings, depreciation calculations, or carryforward activities have already been performed for 2027.

If you opened 2027 accidentally but no productive postings or year dependent asset data exist, you can report the incident for further verification to confirm whether the fiscal year can be corrected safely. Do not attempt to change the value through an unsupported interface or custom code because the highest fiscal year controls year dependent Asset Accounting data.

If your postings or asset values already exist for 2027, lowering the year to 2026 is generally not supported instead, you should keep 2027 as the highest open fiscal year, control your actual postings using the posting period settings and keep your 2027 posting periods closed until your business is ready to use them.

The Highest Fiscal Year does not by itself mean that you can post into 2027. Posting authorisation is controlled separately through the financial posting periods.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Material Type Configuration in SAP S/4 system
entry.content:
Price control Mandatory: This indicator when checked indicates price control should be mandatory while creating a material for this material type or if not checked it acts as an optional.

The above statement in the blog is not entirely correct. Below is the SAP documentation regarding the flag

Price Control Mandatory

Use

Determines whether the price control indicator defined for the material type appears as a default value (indicator not set) or a fixed value (indicator set) when material master records are created or changed.

When this flag is checked, users are not allowed to change the price control of any material created with the corresponding material type.

entry.title: Re: 如何通过加密厂商北京前沿信安科技股份有限公司的前置Proxy登陆S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi @JuliaLu 

Short answer: Yes, but only in a very narrow way — the only supported route is the business context Coding Block (FINS_CODING_BLOCK), the field shows only on G/L account line items, and there is a cap on the number of fields you can create.

Which business context to select

Use Accounting: Coding Block (technical name FINS_CODING_BLOCK) in the Custom Fields app. Coding Block is the context whose values can be entered manually on the UI and are then passed into the journal entry and to follow-on documents. Reference: Custom Fields for Journal Entries Used in G/L Accounting

Do not use Accounting: Journal Entry Item for this requirement — values in that context are derived by the system and cannot be typed in by the user. Accounting: Market Segment is only for margin analysis characteristics. Reference: KBA 3041720

What to enable on the UI side

Create Outgoing Invoices is not a Fiori/OData app — it is the classic transaction FB70 rendered as SAP GUI for HTML in the launchpad (SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library – Create Outgoing Invoices). Therefore, it does not appear as a selectable UI/OData service in the User Interfaces tab of the Custom Fields app. After you create the field and publish it, the Coding Block field is picked up automatically in the account assignment part of the G/L line item — there is no per-app "Enable Usage" switch for FB70.

Practical tip: maintain the medium label and short label in the UI Texts section — those are the texts used by SAP GUI applications, whereas Fiori uses label/tooltip. Reference: Editing Custom Fields

What to enable for CDS views / reporting

CDS enablement is not needed to make the field visible in FB70. It is needed only if you want the value in reporting or APIs: open the published field and use the Analytics tab (data sources / CDS views, e.g. journal entry line item views) and the APIs, Form Templates or Email Templates tabs as required, then Publish again. Reference: Editing Custom Fields

Coding Block context overall: maximum 20 custom fields, 400 characters in total, data types limited to CHAR and NUMC, and the fields are relevant for G/L account line items only — not for the customer (AR) line, AP items or asset accounting. Reference: Extensibility – how to implement Custom Fields using Coding Block business context

If this limit does not fit your requirement

Consider posting via Post General Journal Entries / Upload General Journal Entries / Manage Journal Entries, where Coding Block fields are fully supported and can be enabled explicitly under User Interfaces > Services > Enable Usage (same blog above). If your "outgoing invoice" is actually an SD billing document (Create Billing Documents, F0798), the correct contexts are Sales: Billing Document / Sales: Billing Document Item, and there the UI enablement for the Fiori apps does exist: Process Extensibility for Sales Documents and Billing Documents

Summary

Create the field in the Custom Fields app with business context Accounting: Coding Block, maintain the short/medium labels, and Publish — it then appears on the G/L line items of Create Outgoing Invoices with no app-specific UI activation. Use the Analytics tab only if you also need the field in CDS-based reporting. If you need a field on the customer line, Create Outgoing Invoice is the wrong app for that requirement.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Re: Parallel Processing in ABAP using SPTA Framework
entry.content:
I consider that the SPTA framework is obsolete (based on the obsolete subroutines and not officially released for use by customers) but still exists in legacy applications.

The equivalent modern solutions are CL_ABAP_PARALLEL and bgPF.

For more information about all parallel frameworks (including SPTA), see ABAP's Parallel Processing Frameworks: Practical G... - SAP Community.

entry.title: Re: Built an MCP Server for SAP Cloud Integration

The issue we face is with the format of long text. Users plan to copy and paste table from excel into the long text field (word editor) and then it must be printed in the same format. entry.title: Secure Outbound Communication in ABAP Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Modern ABAP development, especially in cloud environments, is increasingly centered around one core idea: while application logic still triggers and executes the outbound calls, the underlying communication configuration and connection settings must be strictly separated from the application logic to ensure secure and controlled integration. Whether an application calls an external HTTP service or an RFC-enabled system, the goal is no longer just to make the call succeed, but to ensure that every connection is governed, traceable, and aligned with clean-core principles.

SAP addresses this through a structured communication model in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP BTP ABAP environment and SAP S/4HHANA Private Cloud Edition.

The model is based on two pillars: The development object Communication Target (CoTa) and the configuration stored in so called Application Destinations.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (and in on-premise), integrations require both the communication target and the application destinations to be maintained explicitly. In contrast, for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP BTP ABAP Environment, the application destination is generated automatically when the administrator saves the communication arrangement, further simplifying configuration and lifecycle management. While the system handles the creation of the destination automatically, meaning customers do not need to write custom code to save the destination itself, users retain full control over where the automatically generated destination name is stored and maintained within the system (see Executing Custom Code During a Communication Arrangement Save | SAP Help Portal )

While ABAP Cloud preserves the principle of separating configuration from application logic and still requires code adaptations when communication protocols change (for example, from RFC to HTTP), it significantly modernizes connectivity management.

Communication targets replace this approach with a formal development object that represents the intended communication purpose. Rather than referencing a technical destination directly, application code refers to the communication target, and the runtime framework resolves the underlying destination. This automation applies when there is only a single application destination, in other words, the application logic remains completely unaware of the application destination name.

If multiple application destinations exist, the specific name of the application destination must still be explicitly provided in the code, just as with a traditional SM59 destination.

Communication targets also act as a logical control layer that governs access to destinations. Unlike the traditional model, where any application could potentially reuse a destination if its name was known, communication targets explicitly define which applications are allowed to use a particular destination. This prevents uncontrolled reuse and establishes a clearer and more governed communication model.

By enforcing this explicit authorization, the model mitigates critical security risks, such as:

  • Data Spoofing & Interception: Unauthorized applications can no longer masquerade as legitimate services to intercept sensitive data streams.
  • Resource Depletion (DoS): Rogue or compromised applications are prevented from flooding a destination with traffic, ensuring high availability for authorized users.
  • Privilege Escalation: It eliminates the risk of an application accidentally (or maliciously) gaining access to high-privilege backend systems simply by guessing a destination name.

More in Communication Target | SAP Help Portal and Using Communication Targets | SAP Help Portal

Although both communication targets and SM59 destinations separate configuration from business logic, communication targets extend this concept through stronger lifecycle integration, improved traceability, controlled destination access, and greater flexibility in managing tenant-specific configuration.

Besides, communication targets deliver key architectural upgrades by utilizing an object-oriented class basis. Because all communication is routed through communication targets, the user can perform reliable where-used analysis and runtime validation. In addition, communication targets allow developers to define general communication settings and client dependency as part of the development object, reducing configuration errors and simplifying administration. These two advantages represent the core improvements of the new concept.

HTTP Communication via Communication Targets

For HTTP communication, this model is reinforced by communication target. Each outbound call is validated at runtime to ensure that the application is authorized to use the destination behind the communication target. This major improvement over the classic model significantly strengthens security by design.

From a development perspective, the beauty of this model is its simplicity in code. Once a communication arrangement is defined and the application destination is generated, the ABAP implementation becomes clean and focused purely on business logic. All technical details such as URLs, authentication, and certificates, are handled outside the application.
In case there will be only one application destination (most likely case), a typical HTTP call in ABAP Cloud looks like below.

DATA: lo_cota TYPE REF TO ZCT_EXAMPLE.

lo_cota = NEW ZCT_EXAMPLE(application_destination = 'Z_APPLDEST_EXAMPLE' ).

DATA(lo_client) = lo_cota->create_web_http_client( ).

DATA(lo_response) = lo_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>get ).

However, if multiple application destinations are available for the Communication target, you must explicitly pass the name of the relevant application destination as a parameter to ensure the outbound call maps to the correct target system.

The code is intentionally minimal. For example, in Cloud, the application. This ensures that the application can only communicate with endpoints that are explicitly approved.

RFC Communication via Communication Targets

The same principle applies to RFC communication: for outbound RFC calls, you should use communication targets together with the application destination. The same principle applies to RFC communication: for outbound RFC calls, it is recommended to use communication targets together with the application destination.
The architectural lifecycle of application destinations is conceptually identical for both RFC and HTTP protocols; in both cases, the communication target serves as the abstract reference in your ABAP code, while administrators manage the destination details. The distinction lies purely in the developer implementation at runtime, where protocol-specific configuration and distinct APIs, such as classic function modules for RFC versus HTTP client classes for REST, must be used to execute the call.

In the newer governance model, RFC connections can be governed through communication arrangements and communication targets, providing the same structured, secure, and centrally managed approach that is already used for HTTP communication. In code, the call may still look familiar:

DATA: lo_cota TYPE REF TO ZCT_EXAMPLE.
    lo_cota = NEW ZCT_EXAMPLE( ).
    DATA(lo_remote_session) = lo_cota->create_session( ).
    CALL FUNCTION 'RFC_PING' IN REMOTE SESSION lo_remote_session.

The important difference is not only in the governance of the destination, but also in the syntax itself, which is an important aspect of the new concept. A session object is created from the corresponding CoTa class. With this class-based approach, developers can perform a where-used analysis on the class. This reflects a broader shift in ABAP development. Developers are no longer responsible for managing connectivity details directly. Instead, they consume centrally defined communication configurations. This separation improves maintainability and allows changes to endpoints or credentials without modifying application code.

Cloud VS On-Premise

In ABAP Cloud, communication target is highly recommended and preferred. Within the Communication Management framework of ABAP Cloud, utilizing a formal communication target is highly recommended and considered best practice. However, while the structured framework is preferred for most scenarios, it is still technically possible to establish outbound connections independently when needed. Developers can bypass the standard communication target lifecycle by dynamically instantiating destinations at runtime using specific provider methods, such as creating a destination directly via a URL or a BTP Cloud Destination.


In on-premise systems, the landscape is even more flexible, as they can also leverage modern cloud connectivity concepts. Starting with the SAP S/4HANA 2025 release, on-premises systems support modern connectivity features, including the use of communication targets alongside application destinations (APPLDEST) for outbound HTTP and RFC communication. Meanwhile, the traditional approach using transaction SM59 remains available, particularly for earlier on-premises releases or classic scenarios.
Existing SM59 configurations can also be migrated to application destination for an existing  HTTP or RFC applications. However, the use of communication targets is not enforced system-wide in on-premises environments, unlike in ABAP Cloud.
By early adoption of communication targets, even in on-premises landscapes, developers can align their applications with a model that is clearly designed for the future, secure, transparent, and ready for cloud-native integration.

Further studies

If you want to explore this concept in detail, SAP provides both official documentation and deep-dive blogs:

SAP Help Portal:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/enable-http-communication-in-your-abap-code
  • Outbound Communication | SAP Help Portal
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/consuming-rfc-service

SAP Community blogs:

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/receiver-validation-http-enhance-your-security-by-controlling-destination/ba-p/13972704
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/using-communication-targets-for-outbound-rfc-communication-in-abap/ba-p/14261486

entry.title: Re: Teaching Students to Work With an Unreliable but Inevitable Teammate: AI
entry.content: Martin, your two points are really helpful. Students do need to stay in control of AI as a tool, and your example about Claude getting the rankings slightly wrong shows why that habit matters. Your broader point about AI reshaping business and IT is also important for how we prepare students. I would add one small refinement. GenAI behaves differently from tools like Excel that follow formulas or execute instructions. It generates data. That difference does not make AI mysterious or unpredictable, but it does mean students need stronger habits around verification and judgement because the output is created rather than computed.

entry.title: Configuring AWS Adapter Authentication: AssumeRole, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity and IAMRolesAnywhere
entry.content:
When integrating AWS with SAP Cloud Integration, AWS Security Token Service (STS) enables the use of temporary security credentials associated with IAM roles. This approach reduces the risks associated with long-lived access keys and improves both the security and manageability of authentication.

In this blog, we will walk through the setup required to enable STS-based authentication for the AWS Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. For the AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity use case, we will use Microsoft Entra ID as an example OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider for the web identity federation scenario. We will also cover IAM Roles Anywhere, which enables workloads running outside AWS, such as SAP Integration Suite, to authenticate using X.509 certificates and obtain temporary AWS credentials without relying on long-lived access keys or third-party applications.

The AWS Adapter supports the following STS authentication methods:

  • STS (AssumeRole) – Allows a trusted AWS principal to assume an IAM role and obtain temporary security credentials.
  • STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) – Uses an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider to exchange a web identity token for temporary AWS security credentials.
  • STS (IAM Roles Anywhere) – Uses X.509 certificates to enable workloads running outside AWS to securely assume IAM roles and obtain temporary AWS credentials.

This blog explains the configuration steps required for all approaches.

Let's start with STS (AssumeRole), which requires the least amount of configuration among the three authentication methods. In this approach, you create an IAM role that can be assumed by an IAM user. The IAM user's access key and secret key, together with the Role ARN, are then used to configure authentication in the AWS Adapter.

1. STS (AssumeRole) Setup

This setup directly links a role to an IAM user. No Azure configuration is required.

  1. Create IAM Role
    1. In AWS IAM, navigate to Roles and choose Create role.
    2. Select AWS account as the Trusted entity type.
    3. Specify the trusted AWS account ID that will be allowed to assume the role.
    4. Attach the required permission policies (for example, AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess).
    5. Enter a role name and any optional description.
    6. Review the configuration and choose Create role.

  2. Update the Trust Relationship
    1. After creating the role, open it in the IAM console and navigate to the Trust relationships tab.
    2. Choose Edit trust policy.
    3. Update the policy to include the ARN of the IAM user (or principal) that is allowed to assume the role under the Principal element.
    4. Save the changes to update the trust relationship.

    Example trust policy JSON:

    {
    "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement":[
    { "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal":
    {
    "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/demo-sts-user"
    },
    "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
    ]
    }

One of the most common causes of authentication failures is an incorrectly configured trust policy. This can happen if the IAM user is not added to the Principal element or if an External ID is required by the trust policy but is omitted from the AWS Adapter configuration.

As the final step, create a Secure Parameter security artifact in SAP Cloud Integration (CI) to securely store the Role ARN Alias. This alias can then be referenced in the AWS adapter configuration.

Next, we'll look at STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity), which enables users authenticated by a third-party OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider to obtain temporary AWS credentials without requiring long-term AWS access keys.


2. STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) Setup

This setup uses Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) as an example identity provider.

Note: Supported identity providers using OpenID Connect (OIDC) can be used, such as Microsoft Entra ID, SAP IAS, or other compatible Web Identity Providers.

2.1 Microsoft Entra ID Setup

  1. Register Application
    1. Go to Microsoft Entra ID → App registrations → New registration.


    2. Provide a name, select the supported account types, and register the application.


       

    3. Note down the Tenant ID and Client ID from the overview tab.

       

  2. Add Client Secret
    1. Navigate to Certificates & secrets → New client secret.
    2. Create a secret and copy the Secret Value for later use.


  3. Set Application ID URI
    1. Go to Expose an API → Application ID URI.

       

    2. Set it to api://<client-id> (This will be set automatically).

  4. Configure Access Token Version
    1. In the application Manifest, set requestedAccessTokenVersion to 2.

2.2 AWS IAM Setup (Identity Provider & Role)

  1. Add OpenID Connect Identity Provider
    1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to IAM.
    2. In the left navigation pane, select Identity providers.
    3. Click Add provider.
    4. Configure the identity provider with the following details:
      • Provider Type: OpenID Connect

      • Provider URL:

        https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0

      • Audience:

        <client-id>
    5. Click Add provider to create the OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider.

  2. Create IAM Role for Web Identity
    1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to IAM.
    2. In the left navigation pane, select Roles.
    3. Click Create role.
    4. Under Trusted entity type, select Web identity.
    5. Configure the trust relationship:
      • Select the previously created OpenID Connect (OIDC) provider.
      • Select the appropriate Audience (<client-id>).
    6. Click Next.
    7. Attach the required IAM permission policies that grant access to the AWS resources your application or integration needs.
    8. Click Next.
    9. Enter a Role Name and, optionally, a description.
    10. Review the role configuration and click Create role.
    11. After the role is created, note the Role ARN, as it may be required for application configuration or role assumption requests.

2.3 SAP CPI Configuration

1. OAuth2 Client Credentials:

  1. In SAP Cloud Integration, navigate to Monitor → Security Material.
  2. Click Add and select OAuth2 Client Credentials.
  3. Maintain the following details:
     
    • Name: <Security Artifact Name>

    • Token Service URL:

      https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/oauth2/v2.0/token

    • Client ID:

      <client-id>

    • Grant Type:

      Send as Part of Body

    • Client Secret:

      <client-secret>

    • Scope:

      api://<client-id>/.default

    • Client Authentication:

      Send as Body Parameter

    • Content Type:

      application/x-www-form-urlencoded
  4. Save and deploy the security artifact.

  5. Reference this OAuth2 Client Credentials security artifact in the sender or receiver adapter by specifying it in the OIDC Credential Name field.

2. Secure Parameter (Role ARN)

  1. In SAP Cloud Integration, navigate to Monitor → Security Material.
  2. Click Add and select Secure Parameter.
  3. Store the Role ARN from AWS and deploy.
  4. Use the Role ARN Alias in the adapter configuration to enable AWS authentication.

 

Lastly, we'll look at STS (IAM Roles Anywhere), which enables workloads running outside AWS to assume IAM roles using X.509 certificates and obtain temporary AWS security credentials without relying on long-lived access keys.


3. STS (IAMRolesAnywhere) Setup

Note: In this guide, Keystore Explorer is used to create the certificates required for the IAM Roles Anywhere configuration.

3.1 Create a Key Pair for IAMRolesAnywhere Authentication

  1. Open KeyStore Explorer and create a new KeyStore; select PKCS #12 as the type.


  2. Click on Tools > Generate Key Pair. Retain default selections of RSA and a minimum Key Size of at least 2048 bits.


  3. Select Version 3 in the Generate Key Pair Certificate pop-up window.


  4. Click on     

     

    icon to edit name-related details.


  5. Click Add Extensions. You can add Certificate Extensions by clicking on "+".

     

3.2 Creating Key Pair: CERTIFICATE AUTHORITY

     6. For Certificate Authority, add the following extensions.

  1. Basic Constraints = critical, CA = TRUE

Note: The path length constraint in the Basic Constraints extension determines how many subordinate CA levels can exist below a CA certificate. Configure this value based on your intended certificate hierarchy.

    b. Key Usage(KU) = Certificate Signing, CRL Sign

   c. Subject Key Identifier (SKI) = NOT CRITICAL

Note: Click on the key icon to select a Generation Method.



  7. Enter the name as ROOT_CA.
  8. Enter and Confirm Password.
  9. You will get a message notifying you of Key Pair Creation.

 

3.3 Creating Key Pair: CLIENT

Note: Repeat Steps 1–5 from the section above, then continue from Step 10 as shown below.

10. For Client Certificate, add the following extensions.

a. Basic Constraints = critical, CA = FALSE



b. Key Usage (KU) = Digital Signature

c. Subject Key Identifier (SKI) = NOT CRITICAL

Note: Click on the key icon to select a Generation Method.

11. Enter the Alias as CLIENT.
12. Enter and Confirm Password.
13. You will get a message notifying Key Pair Creation.

3.4 Fetching the PEM from CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITY

  1. Navigate your ROOT_CA certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Double-click on the certificate > PEM > Copy.
    This will be required at a later step.

3.5 Configurations in AWS

Note: Before proceeding with this section, ensure you have completed the previous steps to create CLIENT and CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITY certificates and copied the PEM.

  1. Fetch the PEM that you copied in the CERTIFICATE AUTHORITY section and paste it in the External certificate bundle while creating the Trust Anchor. For more information, see Create Trust Anchor.
  2. Next, you must update the trust policy to include rolesanywhere.amazonaws.com and create a Profile under Role. For more information, see Configure Role and create Profile.

3.6 Generating the CSR using CLIENT

  1. Navigate to your CLIENT certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate > Generate CSR.
  3. Save it for signing CA later. This will be required at a later step.

3.7 Signing CLIENT using CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITY

  1. Navigate to your ROOT_CA certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate > Sign CSR.
  3. In the Choose CSR dialog, select the CSR created using CLIENT.
  4. In the Sign CSR dialog, click Transfer extensions and click OK.

Note: Transfer Extension allows you to skip the Add Extensions step by copying extensions from the Client.

    5. Next, you will see an Export Certificate Chain ‘client‘. Ensure Export Length is selected as Entire Chain and Export Format is X.509.

   6. You will be able to see a prompt that notifies you about successful CSR Signing.

Note: A Root CA can sign one or more Intermediate CAs, which can then issue client (leaf) certificates, forming a trusted certificate chain from the leaf certificate to the Root CA

3.8 Importing the CA Reply to CLIENT

  1. Navigate your CLIENT certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate >> Import CA Reply.
  3. Select your .cer file and click Import.


  4. You will get the following prompt if the previous step is successful.


  5.  Further, if you double-click on the Client certificate, you can see the hierarchy showing that CLIENT has been signed by ROOT_CA.

3.9 Exporting the Client Certificate

  1. Navigate your CLIENT certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate >> Export Key Pair.
  3. Select Format as PCKS#12 and specify a Password.

Note: You will be required to specify this password during Key Pair upload in SAP Integration Suite.

       4. A successful export will be confirmed by the following prompt.


       5. The final step is to upload the .pfx file to the SAP Keystore. For more information, see Uploading a Key Pair in SAP Keystore.

Note: All IAM Roles Anywhere resources are regional, and they must be created in the same account and region to be used together.

Conclusion

The authentication method you choose ultimately depends on your organization's security requirements and how workloads outside AWS are expected to access AWS resources. While all three approaches use AWS Security Token Service (STS) to issue temporary security credentials, they differ in how the requesting workload is authenticated.

If your organization already uses IAM users and is comfortable managing long-lived AWS access keys, STS (AssumeRole) is the simplest option. It allows you to combine an IAM user's access key and secret key with a restricted IAM role to obtain temporary credentials, making it a straightforward upgrade from static IAM user permissions.

If your organization wants to eliminate long-lived AWS access keys and already has an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider, such as Microsoft Entra ID or SAP IAS, STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) is a better choice. It enables workloads to authenticate using identity tokens issued by the trusted identity provider, removing the need to store AWS credentials.

Finally, if your organization prefers not to depend on either long-lived AWS access keys or a third-party identity provider, STS (IAM Roles Anywhere) provides a certificate-based authentication model. Although it requires additional setup to establish a certificate trust chain, it is well-suited for workloads running outside AWS that require strong, certificate-based authentication without relying on external identity providers.

By understanding the strengths and trade-offs of each authentication method, you can choose the approach that best aligns with your organization's security policies, operational requirements, and infrastructure.

entry.title: How to create Business Partner Master Data to S4Hana Public Cloud from SAP Master Data Governance
entry.content:
Hi,

I want to know how to create/replicate Business Partner Master Data in S4Hana Public Cloud too when Business Partner Master Data is created in SAP Master Data Governance, Cloud Edition.

These are the steps I have done so far.

1. In S4Hana Public cloud tenant, I created Communication User, Communication System using MDG Tenant system id and Communication Arrangement using Scenario SAP_COM_0008.

2. Create Destination in BTP using Inbound Delivery Service URL of SAP_COM_0008 (https://myxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/bc/srt/scs_ext/sap/businesspartnersuitebulkreplic  ) with S4Hana Tenant login credential as basic authentication.

3. I created new Business Partner data in Manage Business Partners - Central Governance app and clicked both Process and Replicate Buttons.

 

Is there any steps I still missed or that are wrong?

 

According to my understanding, that process should be available in the app that is able to do approve or reject.

But, I am not sure in which app I need to check for that approval process and what should I continue to do.

I checked in My Inbox of MDG Tenant and the process does not shown in there.

 

So, could you please guide me the steps I have done for this case is correct or not and how should I continue to do to be able to create that Business Partners in S4Hana Public Cloud tenant too?

 

Best Regards,

Aye Chan

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Data Cloud Architecture: Datasphere, Databricks, and the Unified Data Layer
entry.content:
Hello, 

please stop recommending this:
Design one Datasphere Space per business domain. Each Space is a governed, isolated data workspace with its own access controls.

Its one of the worst advice SAP is pushing to clients. 
Unless you have specific IT deparment for each Business Domain, there is literally 0 advantage to this approach, but it brings so many issues. 

Yes, use multiple spaces when it does make sense, but please do not just follow Business Domain recommendation unless company setups demend so!

entry.title: Re: What is clean core anyway?
entry.content:
I don't work with Clean Core or customers directly, but I have worked in many strategy and architecture roles in the past. The answer to such questions as you raise is always the same:
1. First, all technologists have to understand that we exist only to serve the Business. They make the money, and they spend it on us as THEY choose, not as we dictate.
2. This means that they have to be convinced that a practice like Clean Core is the best way to spend their money. It is not a technology decision; it is a Business decision on how to spend on technology.
3. This means that the Business Case for Clean Core has to be superior to "not-clean-core" and this cannot be established on the basis of a single decision. Faced with an immediate choice and the obvious economy of "just do it!", without the discipline and cost of doing it Clean Core, and without the longer term context that cost justifies Clean Core (the long term flexibility, the reduced upgrade costs, the lack of technical debt and its corresponding interest (yes, all debt has an interest cost, too)) the Business is fully justified in making a pragmatic decision.
4. This means that when a Business makes such a decision, it is the technologists' failure, and not the Business' failure. It is our JOB to know how this works and to make the compelling Business Case for doing it right. It is our JOB to show the Business how to best spend on technology for maximum benefit per unit of cost over time.

When a Business adopts the "just do it!" approach to technology acquisition, development, use, maintenance, and support, it is the technologists that have failed. This failure leads to what I call "The Great Ad-Hocracy": a multiplying can of worms that sucks in increasing resources for less reward over time. The ERP industry prior to Clean Core is a historic example of this kind. That many Businesses still adopted ERP solutions is testament to the enormous value we provide. The point of Clean Core is to unlock more of that value over time, and to enable the Business to retain it rather than spend it on technical debt repayment and interest.

When the Business understands that Clean Core is in their best interests, you will no longer need to convince them to maintain the necessary discipline at every decision point that arises during the lifecycle of the system. They will impose that discipline because they recognise it as the best means to derive maximum benefit over time.

entry.title: Early idea: VS Code extension merging ADT + abaplint, is this useful to anyone?
entry.content:
Hey SAP Community,

I got tired of the usual ABAP loop. Write something, hit Ctrl+F3, wait a
few seconds, see the error, fix it, repeat. So I started building
something to get rid of the waiting part.

The idea is simple: run abaplint locally so you get errors instantly while
typing, and when you're ready, send the code to SAP with one command
instead of switching to GUI.

Right now it has inline diagnostics while typing, basic syntax
highlighting, a code outline panel showing classes and methods, keyword
autocomplete, and an activate command that talks to ADT.

I only tested it against a local mock server, not a real SAP system. Auth
for BTP is not properly handled yet. There's no autocomplete from the
actual system. It probably breaks on edge cases I haven't thought of.

I'm posting because I genuinely don't know if this is useful or if someone
already did this better. If the idea resonates, the code is open and the
architecture is meant to be extended. Take it, fork it, break it, improve
it.

GitHub: https://github.wdf.sap.corp/I749493/abap-adv-linter

Honest feedback appreciated, including "this already exists, look here
instead."

Thanks,

entry.title: SQL Anywhere 17: How can DATABASE SIZE be used with an online dbunload -ao rebuild?
entry.content:
Hi,

I'm using SQL Anywhere 17 and performing an online unload/reload using dbunload -ao.
For large databases, we were advised by SAP to create the rebuilt database with a predetermined initial size (for example, ~15% larger than the estimated used pages) to reduce fragmentation and avoid multiple database fragments.

The recommendation was conceptually:

SELECT CAST(
    SUM(
        TablePages * (PctUsedT / 125.0) +        IndexPages * (PctUsedI / 125.0)
    ) * 1.15
AS INTEGER) AS EstInitDBSizeInPagesFROM sa_table_page_usage()WHERE TableID < 999999990;

followed by creating the rebuilt database with something equivalent to:

CREATE DATABASE NewDB
DATABASE SIZE <EstInitDBSizeInPages>;

My question is:

When using dbunload -ao, how is the rebuilt database actually created internally, and is there any supported way to influence that creation so it uses a predetermined DATABASE SIZE?

If this is supported, what is the exact supported workflow or command sequence? If it is not supported with dbunload -ao, is the only supported approach to perform a manual unload/reload (dbunload + dbinit + reload.sql) instead?

I'm looking for the official SQL Anywhere supported approach, not a workaround.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Solving SmartRecruiter's Salary Calculation Gap for Offer Letters Using SAP Integration Suite (C
entry.content:
Hi Sateesh,

As of now this is by design that the fields will be editable in offer. This is not yet planned in Roadmap.

 There is a reactive way to handle this –

We are completing the calculations in CPI and upserting the data back in SR.

 We can store the values in SFTP or SharePoint for each candidate. (Calculated values from CPI) or may be in staging table in CPI.

Once we create the offer – we can run the get call on offer values and compare these 2 records. ( Stored Offer values and the values upserted via CPI)

If there is any discrepancy – we can use email adapter to trigger email to specific stakeholders or if required – directly action in SR.

Thanks

Kunal

entry.title: Problem with discarding entity using CAP as a proxy of RAP V4 Service
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

i've built an App on the Public Cloud, using RAP V4 Service for data persistence and also using determinations, validations etc.
I'm then using a CAP Service to add custom functionality with accessing external/SAP API's. I added my RAP V4 Service using basic entity projection and a little more complicated event handlers. Most of it works good when consumed by Fiori Elements App, but i am facing an issue, where i discard the entity i am editing (not when creating), it successfully discard its, but then fails to read and rebind to the "active" Entity.

 

From the UI Console and Network Tab, aswell as debugging for hours i'm certain that the discard action works, but the two GET requests fired by FE are running on errors.

My guess to what happens is, that the Draft is discarded before the Read triggers, therefore Entity with "isActiveEntity"=false cannot be accessed and the query fails. The weird thing about that is, that this should be dafault Draft Logic and works everywhere else. So i feel like i am missing something. 

Also i am not using @odata.draft.enabled, because that would then use the CAP Default but we have all our data on the RAP Service and need to proxy the requests the way i do (please correct me if i'm wrong here).

Help is greatly appreciated and i will provide any further information you might need.

Cheers, Henrik

entry.title: Unable to reverse settlement in WBS through CJ88 (error message AA462)
entry.content:
We need to reverse a settlement to two WBS PL/21002/06-02 and PL/21002/06-03. Originally settlement was run through CJ8G t-code. While trying to reverse through CJ88, following error appears-

Quote -

 You cannot reverse this transaction ...

     Message no. AA462

 Diagnosis

     The asset line items belonging to this transaction were already reversed
     or are themselves reversal postings. In either case, the system does not
     allow another reversal. Reversed documents and the reversal documents
     for them are automatically marked as such, and you cannot process them
     further.

 

 Procedure

     Check your choice of document. If you really need to reverse this
     document, then you have to manually post a document with reversed
     positive/negative signs from the original posting (back out the posting)
     rather than posting a reversal.

 

Unquote

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Client 8.10
entry.content:
Great news! We had the chance to test SAP Business Client 8.10 at an early stage and are very pleased to see this release become available.

For our organization and our 60,000 users, SAP Business Client remains a key component of the daily user experience. A large share of our business processes is still executed in ABAP Dynpro based applications, making Business Client the practical bridge between our existing SAP investments and our future RISE and Fiori strategy.

It is great to see SAP continuing to invest in long-established products that many customers still depend on. Modernization is important, but so is providing a sustainable transition path, and SAP Business Client plays a crucial role in that journey.

We are also happy to see that the team continues to focus on stability and quality. In our early tests, the product demonstrated a high level of maturity and reliability, which is essential for large-scale enterprise environments.

Congratulations to the team on this release and thank you for listening to customer feedback throughout the beta program.

entry.title: SAP Document AI Basic UI Subscription Fails in BTP Trial Despite Active IAS OIDC Trust
entry.content:
# SAP Document AI Basic UI Subscription Fails in BTP Trial Despite Active IAS OIDC Trust

Hello SAP Community,

I am trying to set up **SAP Document AI in an SAP BTP Trial account**, but the subscription to the SAP Document AI Basic UI continues to fail.

## Environment

- SAP BTP Trial
- Global account: `e14e****trial`
- Subaccount: `trial_Document_AI_****`
- Region: **US East (VA), AWS**
- Cloud Foundry environment: Enabled
- SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant: **Trial (USA)**
- IAS tenant domain: `ahw5****.trial-accounts.ondemand.com`

## Current Setup

The following components have been configured successfully.

### 1. SAP Document AI Trial Service Instance

- Service: `SAP Document AI Trial`
- Plan: `blocks_of_100`
- Status: `Created`
- Service key: Created successfully

### 2. SAP Cloud Identity Services

- Plan: `default`
- Subscription status: `Subscribed`
- Cloud Identity Services tenant: Activated

### 3. OIDC Trust

The OIDC trust between the SAP BTP subaccount and SAP Cloud Identity Services has been established.

- Custom Identity Provider status: `Active`
- Origin key: `sap.custom`
- Available for user logon: `Yes`
- Protocol: `OpenID Connect`

The Cloud Identity Services tenant settings also show that the BTP global account and the Document AI subaccount are connected.

- Global account identifier: `8b59****153c`
- Subaccount identifier: `95a3****85fc`

### 4. Entitlements

The following entitlements are assigned to the same subaccount.

- `SAP Document AI Trial`
- Plan: `blocks_of_100`
- Subaccount assignment: `1 unit`
- `SAP Document AI`
- Plan: `default (Application)`

The remaining global quota for `blocks_of_100` is `0 units` because the available unit is already assigned to this subaccount.

### 5. Role Collection

The SAP Document AI role collection was also created successfully.

## Problem

When I subscribe to the following application:

- Application: `SAP Document AI`
- Plan: `default`
- Application technical name: `document-information-extraction-trial-application-ias`
- Subscription ID: `28cd****daf1`

the subscription ends with the following status:

> **Subscription Failed**

The detailed message only states:

> Couldn't subscribe to SAP Document AI. Try again. If the problem persists, please post a question to SAP Community.

The **Go to Application** button remains disabled.

## Additional Observations

The Service Marketplace contains two separate entries:

- `SAP Document AI`
- `SAP Document AI Trial`

The `SAP Document AI Trial` service instance using the `blocks_of_100` plan was created successfully.

Only the `SAP Document AI` Basic UI subscription using the `default` plan fails.

In the SAP Cloud Identity Services administration console, only the following system applications are visible:

- Administration Console
- User Profile

No SAP Document AI application appears to have been provisioned in the Cloud Identity Services tenant.

I also deleted the failed SAP Document AI UI subscription and tried subscribing again after the OIDC trust was fully established, but the same error occurred.

## Questions

1. Is this a known provisioning issue with the new **SAP Document AI Trial** setup?
2. Are there any additional prerequisites for subscribing to `document-information-extraction-trial-application-ias`?
3. Is there an audit log or detailed provisioning log where the actual subscription failure reason can be checked?
4. Could the separate `SAP Document AI` and `SAP Document AI Trial` Marketplace entries affect the Trial Basic UI provisioning?
5. Is any additional configuration required in SAP Cloud Identity Services before subscribing to the Basic UI?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: The refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is now available
entry.content:
After months of close collaboration with our partner ecosystem, the SAP Integration and Certification Center (ICC) is proud to announce that the refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is officially live now.

This is a significant milestone — not just for SAP ICC, but for every partner building solutions on the SAP platform. The refreshed framework is the result of real conversations, real feedback, and a shared commitment to making integration certification more accessible, more relevant, and more aligned with where SAP and its partners are heading.

Why We Refreshed the Framework

The integration landscape has changed fundamentally. The rise of Business AI, the shift to Clean Core, the evolution of SAP Business AI Platform, and the introduction of new API policy all demanded a certification framework that could keep pace.

The previous framework served us well — but it was built for a different era. We set out to build something that reflects today's partner reality: diverse solution types, varied maturity levels, diverse system landscapes and an ecosystem that spans from established global partners to innovative niche specialists entering the market.

The result is a framework that is structured yet flexible, rigorous yet accessible.

Two Paths. One Goal.

At the heart of the refreshed framework is a dual-path model — two transparent paths designed to meet partners where they are.

SAP Partner Solution Certification Framework 2026 — Two transparent paths

Of the two pathways, Integration Certification is more closely aligned with SAP product architecture strategy. It is designed for applications built on SAP Business AI Platform that meet a defined set of technical standards: use of published APIs, adherence to Clean Core principles, connectivity to SAP Business Data Cloud, and — where applicable — built with SAP Business AI.

Integration Certification is based on endorsed use-cases and technical criteria by SAP. SAP ICC certifies against a defined portfolio of scenarios available at SAP Partner Portal, meaning the partner application shall map to one of those scenarios to be eligible.

The updated framework is aligned with SAP API policy. A certified solution is not just technically validated — it is a direct confirmation that the solution adheres to the design time guidelines of the new SAP API policy, giving customers and partners a clear and trusted signal of compliance.

Successfully certified solutions receive the SAP Certified logo and a certificate, are listed in the SAP Certified Solutions Directory, and gain access to go-to-market support through SAP Store — making certification not just a quality mark, but a tangible business advantage. Integration Certification continues to be a mandatory requirement for higher solution progression levels in SAP PartnerEdge (Spotlight+, Endorsed App).

Interoperability Review is a new, purposeful entry point into the integration certification framework — and one of the most requested suggestion from our partner and the broader community. Certain partner solutions are not able to qualify for Integration Certification and fit a predefined certification scenario. That's not a dead end – it's what the Interoperability Review is built for.

Interoperability Review is SAP ICC's open and inclusive validation path, available to partners and non-partners (ISVs) alike, regardless of where you sit in the SAP ecosystem. It is designed for applications that use published APIs correctly but integrate via methods or platforms that fall outside the SAP Integration Certification Framework – and it's open to standard, market-ready products.

SAP ICC assesses your application against interoperability standards: correct use of published APIs as documented, successful authentication and connectivity, correct data exchange, and appropriate error handling. The review confirms technical compatibility – without requiring deep platform alignment.

If the partner solution passes the Interoperability Review, the solution is listed as interoperable on SAP Notes, and receive a Review Summary documenting the outcome of the technical assessment. With these outcomes, partners can demonstrate to joint customers that the solution is compatible with SAP suite of solutions.

These two paths are not competing options. They are complementary instruments that together ensure every partner has a supported route to market for their solution through a technical validation by SAP.

What Happens Next

The framework is now live. Here is how to get started:

  1. Explore the two paths — review the full documentation ICC pages at SAP.com and SAP Partner Portal
  2. Understand the Certification Framework Reference Guide and FAQ
  3. Talk to us — contact the ICC team (icc@sap.com) to discuss which path is the right fit for your solution or apply for our services  
  4. Start your journey — the process begins with a short discovery conversation with the ICC team.

A Thank You to Our Partner Community

This framework did not emerge from a room at SAP. It emerged from the conversations we have had with many partners — at partner events, in certification reviews, in feedback sessions, and from the direct input you have shared with us over the years.

To everyone who participated in shaping this: thank you. You will see your contribution reflected in what we have built with the refreshed framework.

We are proud of this framework. We are more proud of what it makes possible for you and your customers. We at SAP ICC are eager to see it go live and really making a change for our partner community.

The SAP ICC Team
SAP Integration and Certification Center

Have questions? Reach out to the ICC team at icc@sap.com

entry.title: Re: LOSC Putaway: Second WT Gets Cancelled After Confirmi...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

#GenAI-Assisted Content

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Troubleshooting Inbound LOSC Process in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM

It seems you are experiencing issues with the inbound LOSC process where the second Warehouse Task (WT) is automatically canceled after confirming the first WT, leaving the Handling Unit (HU) stuck in the intermediate storage bin. Here are some steps and considerations that might help resolve this issue:

Possible Causes and Resolutions

  1. Storage Type Configuration:

    • Ensure that the storage types are correctly configured for LOSC. The intermediate storage type should allow for the transition to the final putaway storage type.
    • Check if the ‘Confirm Putaway’ tab is flagged for the storage type Wrong Completion Status in the Change Inbound Deliveries EWM APP associated with the inbound delivery item. This ensures that the putaway can be completed only on a final bin.

  2. Warehouse Process Type Settings:

    • Verify that the warehouse process type settings are correctly defined. The ‘Storage Control Relevance’ should be set appropriately Warehouse process type for distributive putaway is not defined correctly to manage the transition between storage types.
    • Ensure that the flag ‘Confirm Immediately’ is set for the defined Warehouse Process Type Preallocated Stock in SAP S/4HANA Cloud WM - SAP Community (WPT).

  3. Configuration for Inbound Deliveries:

    • Check if the configuration for inbound deliveries is set to allow automatic creation and confirmation of WTs. This might involve reviewing the settings for ‘Automatic WT Creation at GR’. The putaway warehouse task is created automatically regardless storage type and warehouse process type settings

  4. Intermediate Storage Type Behavior:

    • Confirm that the intermediate storage type is not set to cancel subsequent tasks automatically. This might be a configuration issue where the intermediate storage type is not correctly linked to the final putaway storage type.

  5. Comparison with Outbound LOSC:

    • Since the outbound LOSC process works as expected, compare the configurations for inbound and outbound processes to identify any discrepancies.

Additional Suggestions

  • Testing and Validation: Conduct tests with different configurations to isolate the issue. This might involve changing the sequence of storage types or adjusting the warehouse process type settings.
  • Consult SAP Documentation: Review SAP documentation and community forums for similar issues and solutions.

If these steps do not resolve the issue, consider reaching out to SAP support for further assistance.

 

For more details, see the following information:

[1] Wrong Completion Status in the Change Inbound Deliveries EWM APP
[2] Warehouse process type for distributive putaway is not defined correctly
[3] Preallocated Stock in SAP S/4HANA Cloud WM - SAP Community
[4] The putaway warehouse task is created automatically regardless storage type and warehouse process type settings

Kind regards,

Henrike
